coredata.dc:title: Facile synthesis of carbon dots incorporated carbonized pomelo peel by one-step method for enhanced solar evaporation
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Q.
Ma, X.
Zhao, Y.
Chen, X.
Bai, X.
Chen, D.
Jiang, L.
He, J.
Wang, J.
coredata.dc:description:
Solar evaporation can be enhanced by incorporating carbon dots (CDs) decorated onto base materials. However, existing literature primarily focuses on multi-step synthesis methods involving separate preparation of CDs and base materials. This study proposes a novel one-step method for synthesizing CDs decorating carbonized pomelo peel (CDs@CPP) composites for solar evaporation. The pomelo peel itself acted as the carbon source and base material. To demonstrate the performance of the proposed method, the properties of CDs@CPP were compared with those of another CDs-decorated carbonized pomelo peel composite (CDs-CPP) prepared through the conventional multi-step method. Additionally, the role of CDs was also demonstrated by comparing carbonized pomelo peel (CPP) and its CDs-based composites (CDs-CPP). The resultant three different materials (CPP, CDs-CPP, and CDs@CPP) were then characterized to identify their structure, chemical components, wettability, and other properties. The results demonstrate significant improvements in sample properties due to the introduction of CDs and the advantages of the proposed one-step method. The resultant materials were then tested in solar evaporation experiments, revealing that CDs-CPP observed an increase of around 3 times and 1.5 times in the evaporation rate compared with the pure water system and CPP system, respectively. Notably, these values increased to 5 and 2.2, respectively, in the CDs@CPP system. Moreover, CDs@CPP exhibited a solar-to-vapor conversion eﬃciency of 120%, with an evaporation rate of 2.43 K g ·
m
− 2
·
h
− 1
at one sun illumination. This work highlights the performance of our proposed one-step method and provides new insights for synthesizing CDs decorated biomass composites for efficient solar evaporation.
coredata.dcterms:subject: One-step method
Solar evaporation
Photothermal material
Carbon dots
Carbonized pomelo peel

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (22062026), the Yunling Scholar (YNWR-YLXZ-2019-002), the Industrialization Cultivation Project (2016CYH04), the Kunming Science and Technology Project (2019-1-N-25318000002603), and the Key Laboratory of Advanced Materials for Wastewater Treatment of Kunming. The authors thank the Advanced Analysis and Measurement Center of Yunnan University for the sample testing service.

This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (22062026), the Yunling Scholar (YNWR-YLXZ-2019-002), the Industrialization Cultivation Project (2016CYH04), the Kunming Science and Technology Project (2019-1-N-25318000002603), and the Key Laboratory of Advanced Materials for Wastewater Treatment of Kunming. The authors thank the Advanced Analysis and Measurement Center of Yunnan University for the sample testing service.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Sample preparation and evaporation experiments workflow.
High-resolution TEM images of (a) CDs-CPP and (b) CDs@CPP.
FESEM images with the resolution of (a) 100 μm and (b) 10 μm; samples from left to right: CPP, CDs-CPP, and CDs@CPP.
(a) The proportion of C, N, O elements, and (b) C 1s spectrum; samples from left to right: CPP, CDs-CPP, and CDs@CPP.
FTIR spectra of CPP, CDs-CPP, and CDs@CPP.
UV–Vis absorption of DPP, CPP, CDs-CPP, and CDs@CPP.
Contact angles of (a) CPP, (b) CDs-CPP, and (c) CDs@CPP.
Evaporation efficiency of deionized water, CPP, CDs-CPP, and CDs@CPP under (a,b) 1 sun illumination, (c,d) 5 sun illumination, and (e,f) 10 sun illumination.
The temperatures of pure water, CPP, CDs-CPP, and CDs@CPP evaporation systems under one sun illumination.

head.ce:title: Facile synthesis of carbon dots incorporated carbonized pomelo peel by one-step method for enhanced solar evaporation

ce:footnote.ce:label: f
ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Qian Liu and Xiaoqian Ma contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para:
Solar evaporation can be enhanced by incorporating carbon dots (CDs) decorated onto base materials. However, existing literature primarily focuses on multi-step synthesis methods involving separate preparation of CDs and base materials. This study proposes a novel one-step method for synthesizing CDs decorating carbonized pomelo peel (CDs@CPP) composites for solar evaporation. The pomelo peel itself acted as the carbon source and base material. To demonstrate the performance of the proposed method, the properties of CDs@CPP were compared with those of another CDs-decorated carbonized pomelo peel composite (CDs-CPP) prepared through the conventional multi-step method. Additionally, the role of CDs was also demonstrated by comparing carbonized pomelo peel (CPP) and its CDs-based composites (CDs-CPP). The resultant three different materials (CPP, CDs-CPP, and CDs@CPP) were then characterized to identify their structure, chemical components, wettability, and other properties. The results demonstrate significant improvements in sample properties due to the introduction of CDs and the advantages of the proposed one-step method. The resultant materials were then tested in solar evaporation experiments, revealing that CDs-CPP observed an increase of around 3 times and 1.5 times in the evaporation rate compared with the pure water system and CPP system, respectively. Notably, these values increased to 5 and 2.2, respectively, in the CDs@CPP system. Moreover, CDs@CPP exhibited a solar-to-vapor conversion eﬃciency of 120%, with an evaporation rate of 2.43 K g ·
m
− 2
·
h
− 1
at one sun illumination. This work highlights the performance of our proposed one-step method and provides new insights for synthesizing CDs decorated biomass composites for efficient solar evaporation.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:def-description.ce:para: CDs decorated CPP composites prepared by a multi-step method using a glucose solution
CDs decorated CPP composites prepared by the one-step method

Solar evaporation rate (
K g ∙
m
− 2
∙
h
− 1
)

Weight change of water in the solar evaporation test
(
K g
)

The total evaporation enthalpy of water (
k J ·
k g
− 1
)

The solar power density of input light illumination (
k W ∙
m
− 2
)

ce:section.ce:para: Solar evaporation as a highly effective water treatment technology is widely used in seawater desalination, wastewater treatment, and sewage sterilization [1–3]. The evaporation rate of a solar steam generation system largely depends on the evaporation device design and the associated light-absorbing material [4,5]. Consequently, various photothermal materials have been proposed for solar steam generation such as metallic plasmonic nanoparticles, carbon-based materials, near-infrared materials, and biomass materials [6–11].

Solar evaporation has seen extensive utilization of biomass-based materials due to their abundant availability, affordability, and eco-friendliness [12]. The biomass materials are used either as a stand-alone solar absorber or as a substrate for other photothermal materials in solar evaporation [13–15]. Among the reported biomass-based materials, pomelo peel (PP) has shown desired performances for different purposes [16–18] due to its unique features, including a thick foam-like porous structure and abundant natural chemical components. Notably, the porous structure of PP could act as water transportation tunnels during the solar evaporation process [19], demonstrating great potential for solar evaporation. Additionally, modifications to the structure of PP could further enhance the evaporation efficiency [20]. Therefore, creating a more porous structure of processed PP would facilitate water transmission and potentially achieve a desirable water evaporation rate.

Carbon dots (CDs) have received increasing attention in solar evaporation in terms of their broad light absorption, surface functionalization, and excellent photothermal conversion eﬃciency [21–23]. The light absorption range of CDs can span from 200
u m
- 800
u m
, making them advantageous for enhancing solar energy adsorption [24,25]. Surface functionalization of CDs is achieved through an abundance of hydrophilic groups in CDs [26], which can enhance surface wettability and, therefore, promote water transmission. Furthermore, lattice vibration generates thermal energy upon light exposure, improving photothermal conversion eﬃciency during solar evaporation [27,28]. Taking the advantage of their favorable characteristics, CDs are typically combined with other base materials for solar evaporation [27]. For example, a material prepared by decorating CDs on a TiO2 nanotube array for solar evaporation exhibited superior performance compared to samples without CDs [29]. Moreover, biomass-CDs composites have also demonstrated excellent solar evaporation rates [30].

However, in the existing literature, the production of biomass-carbon dots (CDs) composites involves separate preparation of the base material and CDs, followed by their combination [24,31]. Specifically, the CDs are synthesized by using conventional methods (e.g., top-down methods or bottom-up methods) [32,33] and subsequently coated onto a pretreated base material [28]. It is worth noting that the CDs synthesis process usually requires additional carbon sources such as lignin, xylose, and glucose [21,34,35]. Moreover, the combination of CDs and base materials is typically achieved through stirring and dripping [30], which may compromise the stability of the target composites. Therefore, it is crucial to propose a one-step method for synthesizing biomass-CDs composites that eliminates the need for extra carbon sources for solar evaporation.

In this work, we proposed a novel one-step method for synthesizing CDs decorating carbonized PP composites (CDs@CPP) for solar evaporation. The carbonization of PP and preparation of CDs were implemented using a hydrothermal treatment. In this process, PP itself acted as the base material and carbon source, and thus the recombination of CDs and CPP was achieved in one step. The as-prepared material (CDs@CPP) was tested for solar evaporation rate and photothermal conversion efficiency. To assess the performance of the proposed one-step method, the same CDs-based composites (CDs-CPP) were also synthesized using the conventional multi-step approach and evaluated in the same aforementioned tests. Additionally, the properties and evaporation experiments of the carbonized PP composite without CDs (CPP) were compared with those of the CDs-based composites (CDs-CPP) to assess the effectiveness of the CDs. These findings contribute valuable insights into the facile synthesis and practical application of CDs decorating biomass materials for solar evaporation applications.

Pomelo peel (PP) obtained from a local market was used as the primary biomass material. Deionized water, obtained using a laboratory pure water machine, was utilized throughout the experimental procedures. To facilitate a comparative evaluation of the proposed one-step method, glucose (AR grade) was employed as an additional carbon source in the conventional multi-step approach. The glucose was obtained from Tianjin Fengchuan Chemical Reagent Technology Co., Ltd. For measuring the mass changes, an electronic scale was employed, while a thermal imager was used to monitor temperature variations during the solar evaporation experiments. The detailed specifications, accuracy, and measuring range of both machines can be found in the supporting information section.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of the CPP, CDs-CPP, and CDs@CPP
ce:section.ce:para:
The pomelo peel was cut into pieces (4 × 4 × 1
c m
3
) and dried under sunlight. Subsequently, 2
g
of the dried pomelo peel (DPP) was mixed with 60
m L
deionized water and heated at 200
°C
for 24
h r s
in a hydrothermal autoclave reactor. The obtained material was then subjected to freeze-drying for 48
h r s
to prepare CDs decorating carbonized PP composites (CDs@CPP). Such a freeze-drying process could ensure the structural stability and nanoporous properties of the target materials. To evaluate the performance of CDs, the same carbonized PP without CDs was synthesized. Specifically, carbonized PP (CPP) was synthesized by calcinating dried pomelo peel in N2 for 4
h r s
at 700 °C.

Furthermore, in order to compare and assess the efficacy of the proposed one-step method, a multi-step method was also employed. This multi-step method involved incorporating CDs into CPP with the utilization of an additional carbon source, specifically glucose. The complete synthesis process for this multi-step method is outlined in the Supporting Information. The resulting composite from the multi-step method was denoted as CDs-CPP, distinguishing it from the composite produced through the proposed one-step method.

For the solar evaporation experiment, a solar simulator (as depicted in Figure S1a) was utilized. Three different types of samples were tested, namely CPP, CDs-CPP, and CDs@CPP (Figure S1b). These materials were cut into pie shapes and immersed in a container of water for solar evaporation. To insulate against heat loss, the water container (a glass bottle) was enveloped in cotton fibers. Solar evaporation rates were measured under different sunlight irradiation intensities (1
k W ·
m
− 2
, 5
k W ·
m
− 2
, and 10
k W ·
m
− 2
). The mass change (
Δ m
) of the water during the solar evaporation process was recorded at 15-min intervals using an analytical electronic balance, while the temperature of the materials (
T
) was monitored with an infrared imaging thermometer. The evaporation rate (
m ˙
) was calculated as follows:
m ˙
=
Δ m
T · S
where
m ˙
represents the evaporation rate in K g ·
m
− 2
·
h
− 1
,
Δ m
is the weight change of the water in
K g
;
T
is time in unit of
h o u r
, and
S
denotes the cross-sectional area of the corresponding material in
m 2
. Note that the diameter of each material is 0.01
m
and thus the
S
remains constant in this study.

The properties of the three different materials were investigated through various analytical techniques. Morphology and microstructure were examined using field-emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM, Nova Nano SEM 450) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM, Hitachi H-800), while the chemical composition was analyzed by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, K-Alpha+) and Fourier Transform Infrared Spectrometer (FTIR, Nicoletis10). The light absorption characteristics of the samples were measured using an Ultraviolet–visible Spectrophotometer (UV–vis, U-4100), while the contact angle of the materials was observed using a contact angle meter (DCAT21).

This section examines the effects of CDs by conducting a comparative analysis of the properties of CPP and CPP-CDs. Additionally, the proposed one-step method was assessed by comparing the performance of CPP-CDs with that of CPP@CDs.

The resultant materials (CPP, CPP-CDs, and CDs@CPP) through two distinct processes were presented in Fig. 1 . It is widely acknowledged that the carbonization process alone will not yield CDs [36]. To verify the presence of CDs in CDs@CPP and CDs-CPP, high-resolution TEM was utilized to examine their microstructure and lattice structure, as depicted in Fig. 2 . The images reveal the existence of clear microscopic particles in CDs@CPP and CDs-CPP, with a lattice spacing of approximately 0.2 nm. These findings provide compelling evidence that CDs have indeed been successfully decorated on the surface of CDs@CPP and CDs-CPP, as previously reported in the literature [28,35]. The lattice vibration of CDs can generate thermal energy when exposed to sunlight, resulting in an increased photothermal conversion efficiency for solar evaporation [37].

FESEM was used in the morphology analysis and the corresponding results were presented in Fig. 3 . Due to the utilization of the same carbonization process, both CPP and CDs-CPP demonstrate similar pore structures, indicating that the incorporation of CDs does not impact the structure of CDs-CPP. However, CDs@CPP exhibits numerous micro-scale pores on its surface, which can be attributed to the hydrothermal treatment involved in the proposed one-step method. These porous structures of CDs@CPP can act as water vapor tunnels, which can potentially enhance water transmission and absorption during the solar evaporation process [38,39]. Additionally, FESEM images of dried PP (Fig. S2) indicated a smooth surface of dried PP without any further treatment.

The presence and difference content of hydrophilic groups in CPP, CDs-CPP, and CDs@CPP is confirmed through FTIR spectra (Fig. 5 ). It is observed that the spectra for all three materials exhibit two clear peaks at approximately 1631.38
c
m
− 1
and 3424.42
c
m
− 1
, which correspond to the carbonyl group (-CO) and hydroxyl groups (-OH), respectively.

However, the peak intensities in the spectral range of 1000–2000
c
m
− 1
and 3000–3800
c
m
− 1
were found to be higher in CDs-CPP and CDs@CPP than in CPP, indicating the presence of a greater number of hydrophilic groups [19] in CDs-CPP due to the incorporation of CDs. These findings indicate that the proposed one-step method has a similar effect as the multi-step methods in increasing the number of hydrophilic groups. The presence of oxygen-containing groups, as identified in the FTIR spectra, is known to enhance the hydrophilicity of the prepared materials. Consequently, the materials incorporated with carbon dots (CDs-CPP and CDs@CPP) exhibit improved hydrophilic properties compared to CPP [19,43].

The ability of a material to absorb light is also a crucial property for characterizing solar evaporation material. The corresponding light absorption of the three different samples measured by a UV–vis–NIR spectrophotometer were depicted in Fig. 6 . Note that the light absorption of DPP is also included in the figure for comparison. The results indicate that CPP and CDs-CPP exhibit similar absorption abilities across the entire wavelength range. This similarity can be attributed to the carbonization treatment, which imparts a dark black color to the resulting samples.

The wettability of CPP, CDs-CPP, and CDs@CPP was analyzed by measuring their contact angles. The corresponding results were depicted in Fig. 7 . The results showed that the contact angle on the surface of CPP was measured to be 58.2° after 5 s, and the water droplet did not fully impregnate within 10s (Fig. 7a). In contrast, CDs-CPP and CDs@CPP exhibited significantly improved wettability, with water fully impregnating in a very short time. Specifically, CDs-CPP and CDs@CPP exhibited water-impregnating times of 1s and 0.5s, respectively. This phenomenon suggests that the incorporation of carbon dots (CDs) has led to an increase in the number of hydrophilic groups, thereby enhancing the hydrophilicity of the CDs-based composites (CDs-CPP and CDs@CPP) [45]. Additionally, the shortest water-impregnating time (within 0.5s) observed on CDs@CPP demonstrated the superior wettability improvement achieved using our proposed one-step method.

To assess the solar evaporation performance of prepared materials, quantitative experiments were conducted using a solar simulator-based apparatus (Fig. S1). The solar evaporation rates of CPP, CDs-CPP, and CDs@CPP were measured under different sunlight simulation conditions and are presented in Fig. 8 . In this paper, the sunlight simulation conditions encompassed four levels of illumination: 1 sun, 5 suns, and 10 suns. It is important to highlight that the evaporation experiments conducted under the 5 sun and 10 sun illumination conditions were deliberately designed to evaluate the stability of carbon dots in varying sunlight intensities. For comparison purposes, the solar evaporation rates were also measured for a pure water system without any photothermal conversion material. It is important to note that the samples were not obtained and tested in the same batch, resulting in slight variations in the measured evaporation rates for the same system (e.g., pure water system and CPP system in Fig. 8a and b). However, the results presented in each figure for different samples were measured under the same conditions. Therefore, the performance of the one-step method was evaluated by comparing the magnitude of the evaporation improvement of CDs-CPP and CDs@CPP based on the pure water system.

The results presented in Fig. 8 demonstrate a clear increase in the evaporation rate of pure water, CPP, and CDs-CPP under the same sunlight illumination conditions. Specifically, under one sunlight simulation, the evaporation rates of pure water, CPP, and CDs-CPP were measured to be 0.50
K g ·
m
− 2
·
h
− 1
, 1.04
K g ·
m
− 2
·
h
− 1
, and 1.55
K g ·
m
− 2
·
h
− 1
, respectively (Fig. 8a). This indicates that CDs-CPP exhibited around 3-fold and 1.5-fold increases in the evaporation rate compared to the pure water system and CPP system, respectively. These values increased to 5 and 2.2, respectively, in the CDs@CPP system under one sunlight simulation (Fig. 8b). Similar increasing trends were also observed under other sunlight conditions (Fig. 8c,d,e,f). Furthermore, the corresponding temperature of each system under one sunlight simulation was also presented in Fig. 9 , which followed the same increasing trend as the evaporation rate.

In comparison to CPP, the lattice vibrations between CDs in CDs-CPP generate heat [37], resulting in a higher temperature (as shown in Fig. 9). This increase in temperature promotes the solar steam generation and subsequently increases the water evaporation rate in CDs-CPP. Additionally, the presence of CDs results in an increased number of hydrophilic groups, as depicted in Fig. 5, which contributes to the higher hydrophilicity observed in CDs-CPP (Fig. 7b). These hydrophilic properties enhance water transportation and subsequently contribute to an increased water evaporation rate.

In addition, our findings suggest that CDs@CPP demonstrates a more efficient improvement in evaporation rate compared to CDs-CPP, with CDs@CPP exhibiting the highest temperature (as shown in Fig. 9). This can be attributed to the fact that CDs@CPP possesses the most porous structure (Fig. 3b), which increases the chance of sunlight refraction and absorption within itself, subsequently increasing the temperature of the system. Additionally, the wettability characterization indicates that CDs@CPP exhibits the highest hydrophilicity (Fig. 7c), which directly correlates with an enhanced water evaporation rate. These results suggest that the proposed one-step method could outperform conventional multi-step methods. In addition, we find CPP@CDs is the optimal evaporator, which also exhibits around 4 times and 2 times the evaporation rates compared with pure water and CPP under 1 sun illumination in repeat experiments as shown in Fig. S4.

Furthermore, in order to estimate the practical application of prepared materials, the evaporation rates of three systems (CPP, CDs-CPP, and CDs@CPP) under natural sunlight are also tested in Fig. S5. As shown in Fig. S5, the highest evaporation rate of CDs@CPP is 0.28
K g ·
m
− 2
·
h
− 1
which is higher than CDs-CPP (0.25
K g ·
m
− 2
·
h
− 1
), CPP (0.22
K g ·
m
− 2
·
h
− 1
), and pure water (0.19
K g ·
m
− 2
·
h
− 1
). The low evaporation rate under natural sunlight may due to the low, unstable sunlight density, surrounding temperature, and ambient humidity. Considering the CDs@CPP shows the highest evaporation efficiency, we prolong the solar experimental duration of 6 h with 1 h interval under 1 sun illumination to ensure long-term stability for practical applications in Fig. S6. And we find CDs@CPP keeps a stable, high evaporation rate with around 2
K g ·
m
− 2
·
h
− 1
, which provides potential for long-term application.

To further demonstrate the superior performance of CDs@CPP, we compared its evaporation rate at one sunlight illumination condition with that of reported PP materials [19,38,45] and carbon dots decorated materials [30,46]. As depicted in Fig. S3, the evaporation rate of CDs@CPP was measured as 2.43
K g ·
m
− 2
·
h
− 1
, which is higher than the reported materials, namely 1.95
K g ·
m
− 2
·
h
− 1
for fractal carbonized pomelo peels (FCPP), 11.39
K g ·
m
− 2
·
h
− 1
for CPP with three-channel waterways (CPP3), and 1.22
K g ·
m
− 2
·
h
− 1
for the polypyrrole-functionalized pomelo peel (FPyPP). In addition, carbon dots attached to the surface modified coconut fiber (SCF) exhibits a 1.5
K g ·
m
− 2
·
h
− 1
evaporation rate under 1
k W ·
m
− 2
illumination. And the utilization of wood as base material decorated by carbon dots (LCQDs-DW) also shows a low evaporation rate (1.18
K g ·
m
− 2
·
h
− 1
) compared with CDs@CPP. These results clearly demonstrate the remarkable performance of CDs@CPP in solar steam generation and highlight the potential of the one-step method for practical applications.

The energy solar-thermal efficiency of the CDs@CPP system was evaluated to further assess its performance in solar evaporation. The photothermal conversion efficiency is a crucial parameter used to estimate the effectiveness of a material in solar evaporation. It can be determined based on the solar evaporation rates, the temperature of the evaporation system, and the input solar power density [47]. The photothermal conversion efficiency (
η
) was calculated using the following equation:
η =
m ˙
h
L V
I
where
m ˙
is the mass ﬂux,
h
L V
is the total enthalpy of the liquid steam phase change and
I
is the solar power density of input sunlight illumination density [48,49]. CDs@CPP exhibits an enhanced capability to absorb a wider range of the solar spectrum due to the decoration of carbon dots, encompassing both visible and infrared light [50]. This broader range of solar radiation absorption enables higher energy absorption compared to incident light energy alone. Additionally, the porous structure of CDs@CPP facilitates multiple reflections of absorbed sunlight, leading to a solar-to-vapor energy transfer efficiency that surpasses 100%, exceeding previous expectations [51]. The photothermal conversion efficiency of the CDs@CPP evaporation system was found to be 120% under 1 sun illumination based on Fig. 9. In addition, The temperature changes of CDs@CPP under 5 sun and 10 sun are also shown in Fig. S7 and Fig. S8. Thus, the solar-thermal efficiencies of the CDs@CPP evaporation system under 5 sun illumination and 10 sun illumination were approximately 159% and 148%, respectively. These results demonstrate the outstanding solar-thermal performance of the CDs@CPP system, highlighting its potential for use in solar steam generation applications.

In this study, we present a facile and cost-effective one-step approach for the preparation of CDs decorated carbonized pomelo peel composites (CDs@CPP) for use in solar evaporation. This method involves the simultaneous synthesis of CDs and CPP, as well as the assembly of CDs in CPP. Additionally, PP itself serves as the carbon source for CDs production and as the base material for the system.

Characterization analyses indicate that CDs@CPP, synthesized using our proposed method, possesses the most porous structure and exhibits the greatest wettability improvement compared to CPP and CDs-CPP composites. These structural and compositional advantages contribute to the superior performance of CDs@CPP in solar evaporation experiments, resulting in an approximately 5-fold increase in the evaporation rate compared to pure water and a 2.2-fold increase compared to CPP alone.

Specially, CDs@CPP achieved an impressive solar evaporation rate of 2.43 K g ·
m
− 2
·
h
− 1
and a photothermal conversion efficiency of up to 120% under one sun illumination in solar evaporation experiments. In addition, our results also indicate that the introduction of CDs not only enhances wettability due to their hydrophilic groups, but also generates heat via lattice vibrations, which promotes solar steam generation. Our work provides a valuable attempt into the development of biomass-derived solar-thermal materials incorporating CDs. This novel approach offers a new avenue for synthesizing CDs@carbonized biomass composites, which put forward an application potential in the field of solar evaporation systems.

Jiaqiang Wang and Liang Jiang conceived the idea, supervised all aspects of this project, contributed to the paper, and wrote the manuscript. Qian Liu designed the experiments, carried out the part of experiments, and wrote the major part of the manuscript. Xiaoqian Ma carried out a major part of the experiments. They analyzed the data and were involved in discussions, critical assessment, and manuscript improvements. Yi Zhao, Xiaohong Chen, Xiaoli Bai, Daomei Chen, Jiao He synthesized and characterized the samples. All authors read and approved the manuscript.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (22062026), the Yunling Scholar (YNWR-YLXZ-2019-002), the Industrialization Cultivation Project (2016CYH04), the Kunming Science and Technology Project (2019-1-N-25318000002603), and the Key Laboratory of Advanced Materials for Wastewater Treatment of Kunming. The authors thank the Advanced Analysis and Measurement Center of Yunnan University for the sample testing service.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following is the Supplementary data to this article.
Multimedia component 1 Multimedia component 1

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.mtsust.2023.100442.
